questions.prompt: Which is the canonical production pattern for using Cloudflare?

choices.text: Run your entire app on Cloudflare; replace AWS/GCP.

Cloudflare in front of your real app on a cloud provider. Cloudflare handles edge concerns (DDoS, global latency, caching, security, some logic via Workers); origin handles application state, complex compute, primary databases. Best of both architectures.

questions.explanation: The canonical pattern is 'Cloudflare in front of origin.' Edge layer for performance + security; origin layer for application logic. Cloudflare-native (everything on Workers/Pages/D1/R2) works for specific workloads but isn't universal.

questions.prompt: A team tried to run their primary Postgres-backed application entirely on Workers + D1. D1 couldn't handle their write throughput; complex queries didn't fit. What's the right move?

choices.text: Move primary DB to a real Postgres on a managed cloud (Supabase, RDS, Neon, etc.); keep Cloudflare in front for edge concerns and Workers for routing/edge logic. Hybrid is correct for most real workloads — D1 is excellent for read-heavy, low-write patterns but not a Postgres replacement at scale.

questions.explanation: Cloudflare-native works for greenfield apps with simple needs. Complex data models, high write throughput, or relational workloads still want a real database. Hybrid is the pragmatic answer: Cloudflare edge + real cloud origin/DB.
